transcript: In the middle of bustling trains, crowded crossings, and packed city streets, many people in Japan are quietly struggling with loneliness. The country has the oldest population in the world with nearly 30% of people aged 65 or older. And as younger generations move away from work and families become smaller, more elderly Japanese are being left to live and die alone. [music] Government figures show nearly 77,000 people were found dead alone in their homes last year. Some undiscovered for days, even months. It's known in Japanese as kodokushi, or lonely death. Toyama Heights in Tokyo is one of Japan's oldest public housing estates, where several such cases have been reported. 88-year-old Setsuko Kurahara has been living alone here for more than 15 years. Her children are grown up and live far away. She spends most days with other elderly residents who live in the estate. >> I have some incurable diseases and I'm constantly ill. I have to go to the hospital a lot. If I can't walk anymore, I'll go to a nursing home. But right now, I'll stay here with everyone. >> One unlikely support network is a company that delivers yogurt every day. For some seniors, the brief doorstep conversation may be the only human contact they have all day. >> They really look forward to us coming and they wait patiently for us in the morning. If they seem unwell, we don't want them to collapse, so we report it to the company immediately in those situations. >> But loneliness isn't just a problem among the elderly. Experts say social isolation is also increasing among younger generations facing intense work pressure, shrinking social circles, and increasing emotional disconnection. It's become so widespread that Japan created a Minister of Loneliness position in 2021, tasked with combating isolation. Some people seek help through hotlines and mental health support applications. It's created an industry worth billions of dollars. >> One example of that is a range of online platforms where users can book a time with a conversation partner, someone to talk to either by video or phone, not for counseling, but simply for company. In a country where many people say they struggle to find regular social contact, it turns conversation itself into something that can be bought on demand. >> The loneliness crisis is already reshaping the way people live, work, and interact with each other. But without deeper social connections, isolation could become one of Japan's defining challenges in the years to come. >> Patrick Fok, Al Jazeera, Tokyo.

Here are some of the biggest mistakes nurses and phlebotomists make whenever they're drawing blood. So, the first thing is that after they clean the site, they let it dry, and then they think, "Okay, I think I forgot where that vein is. Let me just touch it one more time." But, the thing is when they touch it, they contaminate the site again. So, just always remember once you've cleaned it, it's off limits. You can no longer touch it. The next mistake has to deal with the fist. So, whenever you're drawing blood, you have to make sure your patient isn't doing certain things with their fist because alter their lab results. For instance, one thing you don't want them to do is just opening and closing the fist because that has shown to cause hemoconcentration, which can alter hematocrit and potassium. Also, if the patient holds a very tight fist throughout the whole procedure, that can cause it as well. So, don't do those two things. However, most guidelines allow for a gentle light fist to be done, but that fist has to be released once blood flow is established. Another mistake is that they don't seat the needle. So, after you insert and you get that flash of blood to let you know, "Hey, you've hit the vein." You don't want to just start popping on tubes. You have to take a little extra step and seat that needle. So, you're going to gently lower your angle and go in just a little bit like the thickness of a nickel. And this is going to help prevent hemolysis of your lab sample and prevent collapsing that vein. Next is not using a waste tube when you need to. So, you always want to follow your facility's protocols on this, but using the wing butterfly blood collection set, you take a peek at that set, it has a long tube, and there's a lot of air in that tube. And some blood tubes, such as like the sodium citrate, has a specific blood-to-additive ratio, and you got to clear that air out and put it into your waste tube, and then you pop on your tube that you need. Another mistake is leaving that tourniquet on too long. So, you always want to make sure you're popping that thing off because if you don't, you leave it on more than a minute, it could alter lab results causing hemoconcentration. So, the best thing is is you stick the patient, whenever you get blood flow established, you go ahead and you pop that tourniquet off. And then we have the issue with not following the order of draw. So, you know, it would be awesome if we could just draw blood and just pop on any tube we wanted, right? No, you have to follow a specific order of draw because you don't want to contaminate with certain additives that may be in other tubes. Now, if you want to check out the actual order of draw, I have it up there. Another mistake is that they don't hold pressure long enough after they are done with the blood draw. So, what happens is that the patient gets a nice beautiful hematoma on their arm where their blood is just leaking into their tissue. So, you always want to take the time to just sit there and hold pressure, especially if they're on anticoagulants cuz they will bleed for a while. And some patients don't really understand this and understand what can happen and why we want to hold that good firm pressure. The next mistake is a little bit controversial. Not everyone agrees on it. So, the mistake would be using concentric circles to disinfect the site. That is how they recommended that we do it back in the day, you know, you start in the middle and you work your way outward. That was like literally drilled in our head. They have changed it to the back and forth friction where you take it and rub it back and forth to kill those germs. However, you always want to follow your facility's protocols and what they want you to do. And then lastly, not letting the site dry long enough after you clean it. So, I know in healthcare we're extremely busy and waiting 30 to 60 seconds for something to dry seems like a very long time, but there's purpose for that. We have to allow the alcohol enough time to kill those germs. Okay, so that wraps up this video. Now, if you want to watch a comprehensive demonstration on how to draw blood from beginning to end, I have a video up here that you can access.

Hey everyone, it's Sarah with registered nurser.com and in this video I'm going to go over family nurse practitioner job overview. In this video I'm going to talk about what an FMPP is, their job duties, educational requirements, salary, and the likes and dislikes of this profession. So, if you're interested in those topics, be sure to check out those videos and a link should be in the description below. So, first, let's talk about what an FMP is, a family nurse practitioner. This is a registered nurse with advanced practice education. They've either obtained a master's or a doctorate, and there's an asterk by that, which I'll explain that here in a second. And they work in a variety of se settings with um the general population of children to older adults. and they coordinate and provide care to patients under a doctor's supervision. Now, in some states in the United States, not a lot, but some states, they can actually work under their their own authority and they don't have to work under the supervision of a doctor. Now, why does this have an asterk? Starting in 2025, nurse practitioners, all nurse practitioners, in order to become one, will have to obtain a doctor of nursing practice, a doctorate degree in order to be a nurse practitioner. Now, if they're already a nurse practitioner, they'll be grandfathered in, but the new nurse practitioners will have a doctorate degree and master's degrees will no longer programs will be offered, and that's starting in 2022. So, just keep that as a side note if you're thinking about this profession. Job duties include they function m much like a primary care doctor where they care for for most of their patients from birth to old age. Uh they generally a lot of them work in clinics. I'll see their patients in the clinic. Uh they provide the routine care. You're going to go to this person your primary care doctor if you're sick need something or your routine annual checkup. They order diagnostics. They refer you to consults if you need to see a cardiologist, endocrinologist, anything like that. They prescribe medications, diagnose you. They assist in minor surgical procedures as well. Now, like I said before, some states can't allow nurse practitioners to practice on their own authority and even allow some nurse practitioners to open up their own practice. So, that's interesting. And um some specialties that nurse practitioners, family nurse practitioners like to work in, they like to work in surgery. uh the emergency room, they go into cardiac, oncology, renal. So, they can go into a variety of different um job settings. So, educational requirements. First, you have to be a active registered nurse with a BSN, a bachelor's degree. Next, you'll need to attend a nursing program with an FMP program focus. A nursing school will offer that. And like I said, starting in 2022, these programs will the masters will no longer be available. And um by 2025, if you're going to go in, you'll have to get a doctorate degree. And then after you do pass that, you um set for your state board exams to be licensed as a reg as a family nurse practitioner. Now, salary. How much do these nurse practitioners make? They make, according to payscale.com, an average of $84,785. This took all the nurse practitioners throughout the whole US regardless of experience where they work any setting added it all up divided it and that was the average. Okay. What are the likes and dislikes? Okay. a nurse practition family nurse practitioners report that they like having autonomy being independent because whenever they were a registered nurse, they knew what that patient needed. They knew what tests needed to be ordered, but they had to go through the doctor call and get an order. And they feel like now they can provide better care to the patient because they have the authority to do so because they can write the order and the patient gets better care and they have more respect since they've earned the title of nurse practitioner. What are some of the dislikes? Some of the dislikes reported is that some of the MPs were frustrated because they say that some of the patients don't truly understand what the scope of a practice of a nurse practitioner is and that they actually function as a doctor and they're actually cheaper on your medical bill compared to what a a doctor charges. And again, they're responsible for the diagnosis and treatment of health and illnesses, disease management, health promotion, illness prevention, and all that. And these individuals perform, order, interpret uh various diagnostic tests. They do have the ability to prescribe medications in most states. With meningitis, mening- refers to the meninges
which are three protective membranes that cover the brain and spinal cord, and -itis
refers to inflammation; so meningitis is an inflammation of the meninges. More specifically, it refers to the inflammation
of the two inner layers which are called the leptomeninges. The outer layer of the meninges is the dura
mater, the middle layer is the arachnoid mater, and the inner layer is the pia mater. These last two, the arachnoid and pia maters,
are the leptomeninges. Between the leptomeninges there’s the subarachnoid
space, which houses cerebrospinal fluid, or CSF. CSF is a clear, watery liquid which is pumped
around the spinal cord and brain, cushioning them from impact and bathing them in nutrients. In one microliter or cubic millimeter, there
are normally a few white blood cells, up to 5. If we look at a bigger sample, like say a
decilitre, then around 70% of those will be lymphocytes, 30% monocytes, and just a few
polymorphonuclear cells -- PMNs -- like neutrophils. That same volume will contain some proteins,
as well, about 15-50 mg as well as some glucose, about 45-100 mg, which is close to two thirds
of the glucose we’d find in the same volume of blood. The CSF is held under a little bit of pressure,
below 200 mm of H2O, which is just under 15 mm of mercury -- which is less than a fifth
of the mean arterial pressure. Now at any given moment, there’s about 150
ml of CSF in the body. This is constantly replenished, with around
500 ml of new CSF produced everyday and the excess, or 500 minus 150 mL or 350 mL, is
absorbed into the blood. But for any nutrients to enter and leave the
CSF, and the brain itself for the matter, they have to go through the tightly regulated
by the blood-brain barrier. The blood brain barrier is the special name
given to the blood vessels in the brain. That’s because the endothelial cells in
the blood vessels are so tightly-bound to one another that they prevent leakage and
only allow certain molecules to slip through them. Meningitis is the inflammation of the leptomeninges,
which remember are the inner two membranes around the brain and spinal cord. It is not the inflammation of the brain itself,
that’s encephalitis; but sometimes they can occur together and when that happens it’s
called meningoencephalitis. So meningitis needs some kind of trigger for
the inflammation, and could be an autoimmune disease, where the body attacks itself, like
lupus, or the body having an adverse reaction to some medication, which can happen with
intrathecal therapy, when medication is injected directly into the CSF. But, by far, infection is the most common
trigger for meningitis across all age groups, like with the Neisseria meningitidis bacteria
or herpes simplex virus for example. Now there are two routes that an infection
take to reach the CSF and leptomeninges. The first way is direct spread, which is when
a pathogen gets inside the skull or spinal column, and then penetrates the meninges,
eventually ending up in the CSF. Sometimes the pathogen will have come through
the overlying skin or up through the nose, but it’s more likely that there’s an anatomical
defect to blame. For example, it could be a congenital defect
like spina bifida, or an acquired one like a skull fracture, where there might be CSF
leaking through the sinuses. The second way is hematogenous spread, which
is when a pathogen enters the bloodstream and moves through the endothelial cells in
the blood vessels making up the blood-brain barrier and gets into the CSF. To do this, the pathogens typically have to
bind to surface receptors on the endothelial cells in order to get across. Otherwise, they have to find areas of damage
or more vulnerable spots like the choroid plexus. Once the pathogen finds a way into the CSF
it can start multiplying. Soon enough, the handful of white blood cells
surveilling the CSF identify the pathogen and release cytokines to recruit additional
immune cells. Over time, a microliter of CSF might go on
to contain up to thousands of white blood cells, but any more than five usually defines
meningitis. In most bacterial cases, there’ll be above
100 white blood cells per microliter, and more than 90% PMNs. In most viral cases, there’ll be 10 to 1000
white blood cells; over 50% lymphocytes and under 20% PMNs. In most fungal cases, there’ll be 10-500
white blood cells, with over being 50% lymphocytes. In most cases of tuberculous meningitis there’ll
be 50-500 white blood cells with over 80% being lymphocytes. The additional immune cells attract more fluid
to the area and start causing local destruction as they try to control the infection. As a result the CSF pressure typically rises
above 200 mm of H2O. The immune reaction also causes the glucose
concentration in the CSF to fall, to below two thirds of the concentration in the blood,
and makes the protein levels increase to over 50 mg per decilitre. When it comes to the causes of meningitis,
viruses and bacteria usually cause acute meningitis, whereas fungi usually cause chronic meningitis. Now for bacteria there are a lot of possibilities. In newborns, the most common causes are Group
B streptococci, E coli, and Listeria monocytogenes. In children and teens, the most common causes
are Neisseria meningitidis and Streptococcus pneumoniae. In adults and the elderly, the most common
causes are Streptococcus pneumoniae and Listeria monocytogenes. There are also tick-borne causes of meningitis
like Borrelia burgdorferi bacteria - which the cause of Lyme disease. As for viruses, the main culprits are enteroviruses,
especially coxsackie virus, and herpes simplex virus. HIV is usually contracted through body fluids
and can also cause viral meningitis. Less common causes include mumps virus, varicella
zoster virus, and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus. There’s also the fungi, like those from
the Cryptococcus and Coccidioides genuses, which mainly affect immunocompromised individuals. And then of course there’s tubercular meningitis
is caused by the Mycobacterium tuberculosis bacteria, and finally parasitic causes of
meningitis like P. falciparum which is the main cause of malaria. Now, the classic triad of meningitis symptoms
are headaches, fevers, and nuchal rigidity, or neck stiffness. It can also cause photophobia which is discomfort
with bright lights, or phonophobia, which is discomfort with loud noises. Meningoencephalitis can cause an altered mental
state or seizures. The diagnosis of meningitis starts with a
physical exam. One maneuver is when a person lies flat on
their back facing upwards, and one of their legs is raised with the knee flexed to a 90
degree angle. Then, the leg is supported and slowly straightened
at the knee. If this causes back pain, then it’s called
the Kernig’s sign. Another maneuver, is when a person lies flat
on their back facing upwards, and has their neck supported and flexed. If this causes them to automatically flex
their knees or hips, then it’s called the Brudzinski’s sign. If meningitis is suspected, a lumbar puncture
can be done. This is when a needle goes through the lower
lumbar vertebral levels of the spinal cord, between L3 and L4 for example. The needle penetrates into the subarachnoid
space and a few milliliters of CSF is taken. The opening pressure can be measured, and
the CSF can be analyzed for white blood cells, protein, and glucose. Polymerase chain reaction, or PCR, might be
used to find Specific causes like HIV, enterovirus, HSV, or tuberculosis. If a particular infection seems like an obvious
cause, then a test for that might be used, like the Western blot for Borrelia burgdorferi
bacteria, or a thin blood smear for malaria. The treatment of meningitis depends on the
underlying cause. For bacterial meningitis, It’s common is
to administer steroids and then antibiotics, to prevent massive injury to the leptomeninges
from the inflammation caused as the antibiotics destroy the bacteria. In general the treatment - antivirals, antibacterials,
antifungals, or antiparasitic drugs are aimed at the specific cause of meningitis. Prevention with a vaccine, is appropriate
for some causes like Neisseria meningitidis, but also for mumps and for disseminated tuberculosis. Prophylactic antibiotics can also be administered,
to avoid outbreaks of bacterial meningitis like in households where individuals haven’t
been vaccinated against Neisseria meningitidis. All right, as a quick recap... meningitis is an inflammation of the leptomeninges,
the inner two membranes that surround and protect both the brain and spinal cord. It normally starts when a foreign substance,
oftentimes bacteria, makes its way inside the leptomeninges, either by by direct contact
or hematogenous spread through the blood brain barrier. The immune system responds to the antigen
by flooding the subarachnoid space with white blood cells, which, release chemokines - and
create inflammation and this results in the classic triad of symptoms: headaches, fevers,
and neck stiffness.

welcome to health and wellness i'm will nurse practitioner helping you level up your nursing and today we're going to be talking about the nurse practitioner specialties what are the differences and which one might be right for you so let's clock in so nurse practitioners are registered nurses who attend additional schooling and training to become advanced practice registered nurses or aprns when a nurse wants to become a nurse practitioner they have to attend an advanced nursing program that grants them either a master's of science in nursing msn or a doctorate of nursing practice a dnp these programs are specific to various patient populations so basically what kind of demographic of patients will you be treating these are known as nurse practitioner specialties it is important to pick the right nurse practitioner specialty before actually going through a program because this can limit your job opportunities depending on what you want to do in the future so knowing the key differences between these specialties is important i want to briefly discuss the differences between these np certifications as well as some general guidance when choosing an np specialty np programs will have similar base classes such as advanced pathophysiology and pharmacology but then each specific program will branch into classes which are specific to that specialty and patient demographic family nurse practitioners or fnp is the most common nurse practitioner specialty accounting for about half of all nps and there's a good reason for that because fnps are the most versatile np certification that you can get fnp programs focus on family medicine spanning from birth to death zero to a hundred plus fmp programs focus on primary care but specialty clinics can be done in the er hospitals or various outpatient specialties this is going to depend on the program due to the lack of age restrictions as well as the general primary care training fnps are able to work in many different settings while most fnps will work in the outpatient primary care office setting many will still work in-patient or in emergency departments or ambulatory care settings like urgent cares the benefit of an fnp working in the er is that they are certified to see patients of all ages many ers have combined pediatric and adult emergency departments and providers are often expected to see both some states and facilities will hire fnps to work inpatient in non-critical care areas this will be state and facility dependent the downside is that while your classes will give you the base knowledge you need for these various specialties most of the hands-on clinical experience will be in primary care and this can leave you somewhat unprepared for certain specialties like the er this makes it important to get a job with a thorough orientation process and great ongoing support by your supervising and collaborating physicians as well as the nursing staff understand that there will be a big learning curve once you get a job in any setting but especially settings outside of primary care and family medicine i personally am an fnp and i have worked both inpatient as well as in the er adult gerontological primary care nurse practitioners or agpcnps is similar to fnps but they are limited to evaluating and treating patients who are usually over the age of 13 but mostly adults in geriatric patients this specialty can also work in outpatient offices including primary care specialty offices and inpatient as well they can also work er and urgent care but they will not be able to see children which many ambulatory care centers will require so they'll have a hard time getting a job adult gerontological acute care nurse practitioners or agacnps or just acnp for short are those who are specialized to treat adults and geriatric patients within acute and critical care areas primarily this means within the hospital both icu and general floor acnps can also work in long-term care facilities like nursing homes and ltx they can also work in outpatient specialty clinics like cardiology gi nephrology etc these positions may have an on-call system as well as hospital privileges which the acnp training really helps with acnp can also work in the er but will be limited to see patients 13 years or older and this can significantly limit the ability to get a job in this setting however the additional training in the acute care setting will likely benefit them seeing emergent patients in the er some programs offer dual fnp acnp certifications which is the most ideal and the most flexible certification or dual certification however it will require additional schooling and clinical hours so essentially you're getting two certifications at the same time a newer certification is the emergency nurse practitioner or enp there are a few enp programs now throughout the us that specialize in emergency medicine these mps will be certified to see patients of all ages in the emergency departments and urgent care settings the education and training will be specific to er and er procedures so while you get great training in that specific area that is your chosen specialty you will be significantly limited on where else you can work other than an er or urgent care type facility women's health nurse practitioner is a certification which focuses on women's primary care needs including well-woman exams reproductive health breast and cancer screenings vaginal and urinary complaints etc depending on the program this may be offered in conjunction with a family or adult geriatric certification as well again this offers great training specific to women's health needs however it will limit you to this patient population unless you obtain a dual certification in something else or later choose to go back and get a postmaster certification but if women's health is 100 where you know you want to work forever then this is a great choice that will give you a great experience in training psychiatric mental health nurse practitioner or pmhnp is definitely gaining popularity and you will be treating patients with psychiatric and mental health disorders across the lifespan this includes treating adhd anxiety depression bipolar schizophrenia and more again this specialty is very specific and you will not be able to work outside the realm of psychiatric mental health however you can work both in-patient psych outpatient psych and even telehealth which is definitely gaining popularity as well pediatric nurse practitioner or pnp certify the np to see patients from birth to 21 years old training and education will be specific to the pediatric population but you won't be able to leave that patient population without additional education and the same goes for neonatal nurse practitioners or nnps who work with neonates infants and toddlers up to the age of two and those are the main np specialties that you have run into as you can see sometimes it is straightforward and other times it's a little more difficult to choose between these programs fnp offers the most lateral flexibility with different specialties and populations but may leave you with a very big learning curve if you work outside the realm of primary care this is a good choice if you're not really sure where you want to work in the future or if you think you may want to do multiple different things or multiple different types of jobs in the future this is also definitely a great choice if you want to work in primary care agpcnp is great if you want to work with only adults in primary care or outpatient specialties acnps are a great choice if you love inpatient medicine and want to work in the hospital or long-term care facility and sometimes outpatient specialty offices the other specialties are a bit more straightforward and will give you great education and training and leave you feeling well prepared in your specific specialty if you want er or urgent care do an enp program if you want women's health do women's health nurse practitioner program if you want psychiatric mental health do a pm hnp program if you want to work with kids do a pnp program if you want to work with neonates do an nnp program however keep in mind that many of those specialties i just mentioned except for neonatal nurse practitioner will also hire fnps or agnps
